threats.title: ETH Value Reuse in Batch Aggregation Allows Draining Router Balance
threats.hypothesis_description: In `batchTransferOutAndCallV5`, `msg.value` is reused for every ETH-based aggregation payload. If a batch contains multiple ETH payloads, the first call consumes the intended ETH, and subsequent calls will either fail or drain pre-existing ETH from the router contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: This could lead to loss of funds if the router holds an ETH balance, or cause batch transactions to revert unexpectedly, halting outbound processing.

proof_of_signal.pattern: msg.value is passed in a loop to _transferOutAndCallV5 without being split

preconditions: A batch transaction contains multiple payloads with fromAsset == address(0)
The router has a pre-existing ETH balance (for draining) or the batch reverts

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: msg.value is not decremented or divided among batch items
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: An attacker could potentially craft a batch (if they control a vault) to drain stuck ETH.

economic_questions: Can a vault intentionally construct a batch with multiple ETH payloads?
How much ETH is typically held in the router contract?
validation_requirements: Test batch execution with two ETH payloads
Verify if the second payload drains contract balance or reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Cost of gas to execute the batch
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Any stuck ETH in the router
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI if stuck ETH exists

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the router has accumulated stuck ETH

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH balance in the router

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Check if fromAmount is used for ETH transfers (it is not)

threats.title: Failed ETH Aggregator Swaps Send Funds to Aggregator Instead of User
threats.hypothesis_description: When an ETH swap fails in `_transferOutAndCallV5`, the fallback mechanism sends the ETH to the aggregator contract (`target`) instead of the intended user (`recipient`). This permanently locks user funds in the aggregator unless it has a specific rescue function.
threats.investigation_rationale: This represents a direct loss of user funds during normal protocol operation if an external DEX swap fails (e.g., due to slippage).
preconditions: User initiates a cross-chain swap to ETH via an aggregator
The aggregator swap fails (e.g., slippage tolerance exceeded)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: send(msg.value) directed to target instead of recipient
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Aggregator receives funds it cannot process, stranding them
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: If the aggregator is malicious, it can intentionally fail swaps to steal ETH.

economic_questions: Do whitelisted aggregators have rescue functions for arbitrary ETH?
Can an attacker deploy a malicious aggregator to exploit this?
validation_requirements: Simulate a failed swap and trace the ETH destination
Verify if funds can be recovered from the aggregator

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: None (user funds are lost naturally, or attacker uses malicious aggregator)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: 100% of the swapped ETH (if attacker controls aggregator)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During high volatility when swaps are likely to fail

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ETH routed through failing aggregators

threats.title: Permissionless swapOutV5 Allows Draining Aggregator Token Balances
threats.hypothesis_description: `swapOutV5` in `THORChain_Aggregator` is public and does not validate that the caller transferred tokens in the same transaction. An attacker can call it specifying an arbitrary `fromAmount` up to the aggregator's current token balance, swapping stuck or accumulated tokens and directing the output ETH to themselves.
threats.investigation_rationale: Aggregators may accumulate tokens from failed swaps (due to the lack of ERC20 fallback in the router). This vulnerability allows anyone to steal those accumulated tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function uses fromAmount parameter to approve and swap tokens already in the contract without verifying caller deposited them

preconditions: The aggregator contract holds an ERC20 token balance (e.g., from failed swaps)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker specifies fromAmount equal to aggregator's balance and sets recipient to their own address
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker extracts 100% of stuck ERC20 tokens in the aggregator as ETH.

economic_questions: How often do tokens get stuck in the aggregator?
Is there any hidden access control?
validation_requirements: Deploy aggregator, send it tokens, and call swapOutV5 as an unprivileged user

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Value of all stuck tokens in the aggregator

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient DEX liquidity to swap the stuck tokens
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After a large user swap fails and leaves tokens in the aggregator

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users who lost tokens to failed swaps (prevents owner from rescuing and refunding them)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All stuck ERC20 tokens in aggregators

threats.title: Aggregator swapIn Attributes Entire ETH Balance to Caller
threats.hypothesis_description: In `swapIn`, the deposit amount is determined by `address(this).balance` after the swap. This includes any pre-existing ETH in the aggregator. An attacker can execute a minimal swap to sweep all stuck ETH in the aggregator into their own THORChain deposit.
threats.investigation_rationale: This allows attackers to steal ETH that may have accumulated in the aggregator from failed transactions or direct transfers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeAmount = address(this).balance; used for deposit instead of tracking swap output delta

preconditions: The aggregator contract holds a pre-existing ETH balance

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker performs a 1 wei token swap, but the deposit uses the entire contract ETH balance
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker receives THORChain credit for ETH they did not provide.

economic_questions: Can ETH get stuck in the aggregator?
Does the aggregator have a receive() function? (Yes, it does)
validation_requirements: Send ETH to aggregator, then call swapIn with a tiny token amount
Verify the deposit event amount includes the pre-existing ETH

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal ERC20 tokens for the swap
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All stuck ETH in the aggregator

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When the aggregator has accumulated ETH

threats.title: Failed ERC20 Aggregator Swaps Result in Silent Fund Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: For ERC20 tokens in `_transferOutAndCallV5`, if the aggregator's `swapOutV5` call fails, the router ignores the failure (`_dexAggSuccess` is unchecked) and does not revert. The tokens remain stuck in the aggregator contract, and the user receives nothing.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a critical flaw in the V5 router design where ERC20 swap failures are not handled, leading to permanent loss of user funds during normal operations.

proof_of_signal.pattern: (bool _dexAggSuccess, ) = aggregationPayload.target.call... return value is ignored, no fallback

preconditions: User initiates a cross-chain swap from an ERC20 token via an aggregator
The aggregator swap fails (e.g., due to slippage or pool depletion)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No require(_dexAggSuccess) and no fallback transfer
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Tokens are transferred to aggregator, swap fails, tokens stay in aggregator
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Combined with ECON-H003, an attacker can intentionally cause a swap to fail (e.g., via sandwich attack) and then steal the stuck tokens.

economic_questions: Can an attacker force a user's swap to fail via MEV?
How easily can the stuck tokens be extracted?
validation_requirements: Simulate a failed ERC20 swap and verify tokens are stuck in the aggregator without reverting the router transaction

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Capital to manipulate DEX pool (sandwich attack)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Depends on the DEX pool depth
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When a large user swap is pending

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All ERC20 tokens routed through aggregators

threats.title: Missing Zero-Approval Blocks Vault Migration for USDT
threats.hypothesis_description: `_routerDeposit` uses `safeApprove` without first resetting the allowance to zero. For tokens like USDT, if there is any residual allowance, the approval will revert, completely blocking vault migration (`returnVaultAssets` or `transferAllowance`) for that asset.
threats.investigation_rationale: This breaks core protocol functionality (vault churn/router upgrades) for one of the most widely used stablecoins in DeFi.
deployment_assumptions: Asset is USDT or a similar token that requires allowance to be 0 before setting a new value
preconditions: Vault migration or router upgrade is initiated
A residual allowance exists for the new router

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: An attacker could front-run a migration with a tiny approval to intentionally block it
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack to halt protocol operations or delay upgrades.

economic_questions: Can an attacker artificially create a residual allowance?
Does the protocol rely heavily on USDT?
validation_requirements: Test `returnVaultAssets` with USDT to a new router

threats.title: Public deposit Function Bypasses Expiry Protection
threats.hypothesis_description: In `AvaxRouter`, the `deposit` function is public, whereas in `THORChain_Router` it is private. Users or integrators calling `deposit` directly bypass the `expiration` check, exposing their cross-chain swaps to delayed execution under unfavorable market conditions.
threats.investigation_rationale: Bypassing expiration checks allows miners or MEV bots to hold transactions and execute them when slippage is most profitable against the user.
preconditions: User or frontend calls `deposit` instead of `depositWithExpiry`

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Transaction held in mempool and executed later
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: MEV extractors can execute the swap when prices move unfavorably for the user, capturing the difference.

economic_questions: Do official frontends use `deposit` or `depositWithExpiry`?
How much volatility is required to make holding the tx profitable?
validation_requirements: Verify `deposit` can be called directly on AvaxRouter

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Volume of deposits using the unprotected function

threats.title: Outbound Fee-on-Transfer Tokens Cause Accounting Discrepancies
threats.hypothesis_description: When transferring out fee-on-transfer tokens, `transferOut` decrements the vault allowance by the nominal `amount` and emits the `TransferOut` event with the nominal `amount`. However, the recipient receives less. This causes Bifrost to over-report the fulfilled amount, leading to L1 state inconsistencies.
threats.investigation_rationale: While inbound deposits handle fee-on-transfer correctly via balance checks, outbounds do not, causing the THORChain state to diverge from actual EVM balances.
threats.config_evidence: Protocol documentation states it expects to handle 'weird' ERC20 behaviors including fee-on-transfer.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _vaultAllowance is decremented by amount, and event emits amount, without checking actual transferred delta

preconditions: Vault processes an outbound transfer for this token

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Bifrost records full amount sent, but user receives less
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Users are shortchanged on outbounds, and THORChain L1 accounting becomes insolvent relative to actual EVM backing.

economic_questions: Does THORChain L1 account for outbound fees?
Can a user exploit this discrepancy to drain other assets?
validation_requirements: Test `transferOut` with a fee-on-transfer token and observe the event logs

threats.title: Permissionless ETH transferOut Allows Event Spoofing
threats.hypothesis_description: `transferOut` is public and payable. Anyone can call it with `asset == address(0)` and emit a `TransferOut` event with themselves as the `vault`. If Bifrost does not strictly validate the `vault` address against an active validator list, attackers can spoof outbound transfers.
threats.investigation_rationale: This relies on off-chain validation. If Bifrost assumes all `TransferOut` events from the Router are legitimate, an attacker can manipulate THORChain's state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Public function with no access control allows anyone to emit TransferOut for ETH

preconditions: Bifrost daemon fails to strictly validate the `vault` address in the event log

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker sends 1 wei to trigger a fake outbound event
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker tricks THORChain into believing an outbound was processed, potentially double-spending or unlocking funds.

economic_questions: Does Bifrost validate the `vault` field against the active Asgard/Yggdrasil list?
What happens if a fake event is processed?
validation_requirements: Review Bifrost off-chain code for event validation logic

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Depends on the spoofed transaction value

threats.title: Phishing Vulnerability in ETH_RUNE via tx.origin
threats.hypothesis_description: `ETH_RUNE.transferTo` uses `tx.origin` instead of `msg.sender`. A malicious contract can call this function when a user interacts with it, silently draining the user's entire ETH_RUNE balance without requiring prior ERC20 approval.
threats.investigation_rationale: Using `tx.origin` for authorization is a well-known anti-pattern that enables severe phishing attacks.
preconditions: User is tricked into interacting with a malicious contract

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Malicious contract calls transferTo during execution
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker steals 100% of the victim's ETH_RUNE balance.

economic_questions: Is ETH_RUNE still widely held?
Are there active phishing campaigns targeting this?
validation_requirements: Deploy a malicious contract and verify it can drain tx.origin's balance
